title: Radians, arc length and sector area

screens.prompt: The sailing race committee wants to mark a $150^\circ$ exclusion zone around the buoy, but the buoy's onboard marker computer only accepts angles in a unit called radians. Do you think $150^\circ$ converts to a nice whole number of radians, or something messier?
choices: A nice whole number, like $1$ or $2$
A messy value: some multiple of $\pi$ that isn't a whole number
screens.reveal: Right, it's messy in general. A radian measures an angle by the length of arc it cuts on a circle of radius $1$, and a full turn measures exactly $2\pi$ radians $=360^\circ$, so $\pi$ radians $=180^\circ$. That gives $\text{radians}=\text{degrees}\times\dfrac{\pi}{180}$, and most 'nice' degree values become fractions of $\pi$, not whole numbers. By the end of this lesson you'll convert freely, and use radians directly in the arc length and sector area formulas.

screens.prompt: Convert $150^\circ$ to radians. Give your answer as a decimal, to 2 decimal places.
Now the other direction. The onboard computer reports a different exclusion zone's sweep as $\dfrac{7\pi}{6}$ radians. What is that in degrees?

choices.feedback: That's the conversion of $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ from the last screen, not $\dfrac{7\pi}{6}$. Recompute: $\dfrac{7\pi}{6}\times\dfrac{180}{\pi}$.
That's double the correct value. Check whether you multiplied by an extra factor of $2$ somewhere.

screens.prompt: Here's $y=\sin\theta$ plotted with $\theta$ in radians, from $0$ to $2\pi\approx6.28$. A full turn is exactly $2\pi$ radians, half a turn is $\pi$ radians. Drag the point to where the curve is falling at its steepest: tangent gradient $=-1$.

goal.description: Target: tangent gradient = -1 (steepest downward slope, at theta = pi radians, half a turn).

screens.successFeedback: Right, the steepest downward point sits at $\theta=\pi\approx3.14$, exactly half a turn. That's the same $\pi$ from the degrees-to-radians formula: half of $2\pi$.
hints: Try moving the point roughly a quarter of the way along the range.
At $\theta=\pi$, the curve crosses zero heading downward: exactly where the slope is steepest and negative.

screens.prompt: Both the arc length formula and the sector area formula need $\theta$ measured in a specific unit. Which one?

choices.feedback: Plugging degrees straight into $s=r\theta$ overshoots by a factor of about $57$ (since $1$ radian $\approx57.3^\circ$). Convert to radians first.
The formulas $s=r\theta$ and $\text{Area}=\frac12r^2\theta$ are only exact when $\theta$ is in radians; a degree value needs converting first.

screens.successFeedback: Right, radians. That's exactly why the conversion you just practised matters before using either formula.

screens.body:
An arc's length, and a sector's area, are both the same fraction $\dfrac{\theta}{2\pi}$ of the full circle. Multiplying that fraction by the circumference $2\pi r$ gives arc length; by the full area $\pi r^2$ gives sector area:

$$
s=\dfrac{\theta}{2\pi}\times2\pi r=r\theta,\qquad\text{Area}=\dfrac{\theta}{2\pi}\times\pi r^2=\dfrac12 r^2\theta
$$

For an exclusion zone of radius $12$ km swept through $\theta=\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ radians:

$$
s=12\times\dfrac{2\pi}{3}=8\pi\,\text{km},\qquad\text{Area}=\dfrac12(144)\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)=48\pi\,\text{km}^2
$$
screens.selfExplainPrompt: Neither formula has a $2\pi$ anywhere in it once it's simplified to $s=r\theta$. Where did the $2\pi$ go?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: It cancelled. $\theta$ measured in radians is already the fraction $\dfrac{\theta}{2\pi}$ of a full turn, multiplied out by the $2\pi$ in the circumference formula. So the $2\pi$ in the fraction and the $2\pi$ in the full circumference cancel each other, leaving the simple $r\theta$. That is precisely why radians, not degrees, make these formulas clean.

screens.worked:
A smaller exclusion zone has radius $5$ km and sweep $\theta=1.2$ radians. Its arc length is:

$$
s=r\theta=5\times1.2=6\,\text{km}
$$
screens.prompt: The harbourmaster also needs the zone's area. Using $\text{Area}=\frac12r^2\theta$, what is the area (in km squared, to 1 decimal place)?

Back to the very first exclusion zone from the start of the lesson: $150^\circ$, which you converted to about $2.62$ radians. If that zone has radius $10$ km, what is its arc length (in km, to 2 decimal places)?
screens.successFeedback: Right, about $26.18$ km. You predicted radians would look messy, and $150^\circ=\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\approx2.62$ radians certainly isn't a whole number, but it plugs directly into $s=r\theta$ and $\text{Area}=\frac12r^2\theta$ without any extra conversion once you're working in radians.
hints: Using the more precise value $2.61799$ gives $26.18$.
